name: Katharine Burr Blodgett
whatOpened: She developed the first practical method to create non - reflective (“invisible”) glass using monomolecular coatings - a breakthrough that revolutionized optics and glass technology, enabling clearer lenses, better cameras, eyeglasses, and optical instruments.
short: Katharine Burr Blodgett was the first woman scientist hired by General Electric (GE) Laboratory and the first woman to earn a Ph.D. in Physics from Cambridge University; she invented “invisible glass,” applying successive molecular-thin coatings to glass to eliminate reflection.
long: Katharine Burr Blodgett was born on January 10, 1898 in Schenectady, New York. She studied at Bryn Mawr College, then earned a master’s degree from the University of Chicago in 1918. Soon after, she became the first woman employed as a scientist at the GE Research Laboratory in Schenectady. In 1926 she completed her Ph.D. in Physics at Cambridge University, the first woman to do so at that institution. On returning to GE, she started working with Irving Langmuir on thin molecular films (surface-chemistry research), which laid the foundation for her major invention. In the 1930s, Blodgett developed a technique to deposit monomolecular layers of material, such as barium stearate, onto glass or metal surfaces layer by layer, a method now known as Langmuir-Blodgett film deposition. By carefully stacking enough of these layers, she discovered how to create a coating whose reflection would cancel out the reflection from the glass itself. In 1938 she patented the process (U.S. Patent #2,220,660) for producing non-reflective “invisible” glass. Thanks to her invention, glass used in eyeglasses, camera lenses, microscopes, telescopes, picture frames, windshields, and many optical instruments became much clearer and more efficient, with less glare, more light transmission, and better visibility. During her long career at GE, which lasted until her retirement in 1963, Blodgett authored numerous scientific articles and held several patents. Her work extended beyond optics: during World War II she contributed to developments such as gas-mask materials, methods to de-ice airplane wings, improved smoke-screen technologies, and coatings for various military and industrial applications. Blodgett passed away on October 12, 1979 in Schenectady. Her non-reflective coatings remain fundamental in modern optics, and her success helped pave the way for future generations of women physicists and chemists.

name: Lydia Villa - Komaroff
whatOpened: She was among the first scientists to show that bacteria can be genetically engineered to produce mammalian insulin - a breakthrough that laid the foundation for biotechnology - based insulin production for diabetes treatment.
short: Lydia Villa - Komaroff is an American molecular biologist who played a key role in the first demonstration that bacterial cells can synthesize insulin, revolutionizing diabetes treatment and helping establish recombinant DNA biotechnology.
long: Lydia Villa - Komaroff was born on August 7, 1947 in Las Vegas, New Mexico. She began her higher education at the University of Washington intending to study chemistry, but later switched to biology; she completed her B.A. in biology at Goucher College. She went on to pursue graduate studies in molecular/cell biology at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), earning her Ph.D. in under advisors including David Baltimore and Harvey Lodish. As a post - doctoral researcher at Harvard University (and briefly at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory), she joined a team that in successfully demonstrated that bacteria could be used to produce mammalian insulin - the first time a mammalian hormone was synthesized in bacteria. This achievement marked a major milestone for biotechnology and diabetes treatment. After this breakthrough, Villa - Komaroff carried out further research using recombinant DNA methods, studying cell biology, growth factors, and developmental processes. Beyond her laboratory work, she has held academic and leadership positions: she worked at institutions like the University of Massachusetts Medical School, Harvard Medical School / Children’s Hospital (Boston), served as Vice President for Research at Northwestern University, and became Vice President for Research and Chief Operating Officer at the Whitehead Institute for Biomedical Research. Villa - Komaroff also co - founded the Society for the Advancement of Chicanos/Hispanics and Native Americans in Science (SACNAS) in - an organization promoting participation of underrepresented groups in STEM - advocating for diversity and inclusion in science. Throughout her career, she has published many scientific works, served on advisory and review committees for major science organizations, and held leadership roles in both academia and biotechnology.

name: Beulah Louise Henry
whatOpened: She invented a wide variety of practical household, consumer and office devices - from a vacuum ice - cream freezer to a bobbin - less sewing machine and a typewriter attachment for making multiple copies - significantly influencing everyday life and pioneering a career path for women inventors.
short: Beulah Louise Henry - American inventor nicknamed “Lady Edison” - held U.S. patents and is credited with over inventions, ranging from appliances and sewing machines to toys and office equipment.
long: Beulah Louise Henry was born on September 28, 1887 in Raleigh, North Carolina. Early in life she showed a talent for creative thinking and invention: childhood sketches already contained designs for mechanical improvements on everyday objects. Between 1909 and 1912 she studied at Elizabeth College in Charlotte, North Carolina. In - while still a student - she received her first patent, for a vacuum ice - cream freezer (U.S. Patent No. 1,037,762), a device that simplified home ice - cream production and used minimal ice. After moving to New York City, she founded the Henry Umbrella & Parasol Company and later the B. L. Henry Company to manufacture and license her inventions. Over the years she developed a remarkable variety of inventions - including an umbrella with interchangeable snap - on covers, soap - filled sponges for children (so-called “Dolly Dips”), dolls with movable/color - changing eyes, and many more. In the 1930s and 1940s, she turned to more mechanical and industrial innovations: a typewriter attachment (the “Protograph”) that produced multiple copies of typed documents without carbon paper, and in - a bobbin - less sewing machine that simplified sewing and reduced tangling of thread. Her inventive output was extraordinary: by the end of her career she held 49 U.S. patents and had more than inventions credited to her - more than any other woman inventor in her time. Even though she had no formal engineering training, her solutions were practical, often born from observing everyday problems, and she worked with model makers and patent lawyers to turn ideas into reality. Beulah Louise Henry died on February 1, 1973. Her legacy remains significant: many of her inventions bettered everyday life and influenced the design of consumer goods, typewriters, sewing machines, toys and appliances. Her success as a self - taught woman inventor - who managed to profit from her ideas in a male - dominated era - opened the door for future generations of female innovators.

name: Maria Cunitz
location: Silesia (Świdnica / Pitschen - then part of the Holy Roman Empire)
whatOpened: Maria Cunitz published Urania Propitia , a simplified, more accurate, and more accessible version of the Rudolphine Tables . This gave astronomers a practical and much easier tool for calculating planetary positions, promoting Johannes Kepler’s ideas and modernizing celestial mechanics in the 17th century.
short: Maria Cunitz was a Silesian astronomer and mathematician who, in 1650, published Urania Propitia , significantly simplifying and improving planetary tables and making astronomy more accessible beyond a narrow circle of scholars.
long: Maria Cunitz was born around 1610 in Świdnica (Silesia). Her father, Dr. Heinrich Cunitz, gave her a private education, because formal education for women was unavailable at the time. Her upbringing included mathematics, medicine, history, and classical and modern languages; reportedly, she mastered seven languages: Latin, Greek, German, Polish, Italian, French, and Hebrew. In 1630, she married a physician and amateur astronomer, Elias von Löwen, who supported her scientific interests. Together, they conducted astronomical observations, including Venus and Jupiter in the late 1620s. During the turmoil of the Thirty Years’ War, including religious persecution and military conflicts, the family sheltered in an abbey, where Maria focused on mathematical calculations. In 1650, after the war, she published her only and most famous work - Urania Propitia sive Tabulae Astronomicae mire faciles - a bilingual edition (Latin and German) containing new astronomical tables, simplified methods for calculating planetary motion, and instructions that made astronomy accessible beyond specialists. In the preface to the work, her husband explicitly stated that the work was entirely Maria’s, confirming that it was not a case of “hidden authorship” by a man, as sometimes happened with women scientists. The scientific community recognized her talent: she corresponded with other notable astronomers of the time, including Johannes Hevelius. Unfortunately, a fire in Pitschen in 1656 destroyed most of her personal documents, letters, observations, and library, leaving Urania Propitia as her main surviving scientific legacy. Maria Cunitz died on August 22, 1664, in Pitschen (Silesia). Despite the fact that women rarely had access to education or scientific work, Cunitz’s contribution was exceptional - her work is considered one of the earliest high-level scientific works authored by a woman that has survived to this day. In her honor, the asteroid 12624 Mariacunitia and the crater Cunitz on Venus were named after her.

name: Domenico Pacini
whatOpened: Pacini carried out pioneering underwater and sea - level measurements of ionizing radiation and demonstrated in June that a significant portion of this “penetrating radiation” could not originate from Earth’s crust - providing critical early evidence that the radiation (now known as Cosmic rays) has an extraterrestrial component.
short: Domenico Pacini was an Italian physicist who, through clever experiments comparing radiation levels on land, at sea, and underwater, made one of the first solid empirical arguments that cosmic radiation comes from outside the Earth, laying key groundwork for cosmic - ray physics.
long: Domenico Pacini was born on 20 February 1878 in Marino (near Rome). He graduated in Physics from the University of Rome in 1902. In the early years of his career he worked as an assistant in studies of electrical conductivity in gases. From around 1905 he was affiliated with the Central Office of Meteorology and Geodynamics - a position he held until 1927, becoming “chief geophysicist.” Later, in 1928, he became professor of Experimental Physics at the University of Bari. Between 1907 and 1911, Pacini investigated the mysterious “penetrating radiation” that caused spontaneous discharge in electroscopes - a phenomenon known at the time but whose origin was debated. In a landmark series of experiments in 1910-1911, Pacini measured ionization rates using an electroscope in different environments: on land, at sea (off the coast near Livorno), and - importantly - immersed several meters underwater. In June 1911, he found that the rate of ionization significantly decreased when the electroscope was submerged (about - 30% lower than at the surface). From these observations, he concluded that a substantial portion of the radiation could not come from radioactive materials in Earth’s crust, but must have an origin independent of the soil - effectively arguing for a non - terrestrial, penetrating radiation. Pacini published his results in a 1912 paper entitled Penetrating Radiation at the Surface of and in Water . Although his experimental approach was independent and complementary to that of Victor Franz Hess (who later used balloon measurements to show cosmic rays increase with altitude), Pacini’s contribution remained largely unrecognized for decades. During his lifetime, Pacini continued his academic work and research in geophysics and experimental physics. He died on May in Rome. In recent decades, historians and physicists have re - evaluated Pacini’s work and recognized him as a co - pioneer in the discovery of cosmic rays; his underwater measurement technique is now considered the first “underground / under - water” experiment in cosmic - ray research, predating many modern methods.

name: Ivan Nikolov Stranski
whatOpened: Stranski developed foundational theories of crystal growth and surface - chemistry, including the “Kossel-Stranski” model and the Stranski-Krastanov growth mode of thin - film/epitaxial growth - work that laid the groundwork for modern materials science, epitaxy, and nanotechnology.
short: Ivan Stranski - Bulgarian - German physical chemist - is considered the father of crystal - growth research; his theoretical models of how crystals and thin films grow remain fundamental in materials science and are widely used in modern nanotechnology.
long: Ivan Nikolov Stranski was born on January 2, 1897, in Sofia, Bulgaria. He studied chemistry and physical chemistry at several institutions including the University of Sofia and later continued his education in Berlin, earning his doctorate in under Paul Günther, with a thesis on X - ray spectral analysis. Early in his career, Stranski focused on fundamental physical - chemical problems, but over time he turned his attention to crystallization, surface phenomena, and the kinetics of crystal growth. In the 1930s, Stranski - together with collaborators - introduced theoretical models describing how crystals form and grow at the atomic/molecular level. Notably, the “Kossel-Stranski model” described how atoms attach to growing crystals depending on their position (terrace, ledge, kink), giving a molecular - level explanation for crystal shapes and growth rates. In 1938 he and Lyubomir Krastanov published a seminal work introducing the Stranski-Krastanov growth mode - a two - stage mechanism for thin film/epitaxial growth: an initial smooth layer-by-layer deposition, followed after a critical thickness by the formation of three - dimensional “islands.” This mechanism is one of the core paradigms in thin - film physics and remains central to semiconductor and nanostructure fabrication. Because of these contributions, Stranski is often referred to as the “father of crystal growth research.” Moreover, he founded the Bulgarian school of physical chemistry, leading the physical - chemistry departments at the University of Sofia, and later at what is now the Technische Universität Berlin - where he also served as rector. Stranski’s work laid the theoretical foundations for much of modern materials science: understanding and controlling crystal growth, surface phenomena, and thin - film deposition - all critical for modern electronics, semiconductors, nanotechnology, and materials engineering. He passed away on June 19, 1979, in Sofia.

name: Gabrio Piola
whatOpened: Gabrio Piola laid foundational principles for continuum mechanics and elasticity theory - his work led to what is now called the Piola-Kirchhoff stress tensor, a cornerstone concept in modern solid mechanics, materials science, and engineering.
short: Gabrio Piola was an Italian mathematician and physicist whose pioneering work on the mechanics of continuous media and the formulation of stress tensors provided the mathematical backbone for modern elasticity and continuum mechanics.
long: Gabrio Piola was born on July 15, 1794 in Milan into a noble family from Giussano. He studied mathematics and physics at the University of Pavia under the guidance of Vincenzo Brunacci and earned his doctorate on June 24, 1816. Although he was offered a faculty position in applied mathematics in Rome, Piola chose instead to teach privately, yet he remained deeply engaged in mathematical and physical research. In 1824 he began publishing a series of treatises and memoirs that applied the analytical mechanics of Joseph-Louis Lagrange to real physical problems - tackling the behaviour of continuous bodies under forces. Through his work, he developed a rigorous formulation of continuum mechanics: he introduced what would later be formalised as the Piola-Kirchhoff stress tensors, offering a “material (Lagrangian) description” of deformation and stress - a conceptual framework still fundamental for elasticity theory, finite - element modeling, and modern materials science. Beyond classical elasticity, Piola was ahead of his time: he explored theories of continua with “higher gradients” (taking into account internal structure, non-local effects, and more general deformation behaviors) - ideas that only re - emerged in the 20th century under advanced continuum mechanics and peridynamics. His writings include many memoirs and treatises (on integral calculus, fluid motion, mechanics of solids, molecular mechanics, and more), reflecting the breadth of his intellectual pursuits and his ambition to ground mechanical theory on firm mathematical principles. Gabrio Piola passed away in 1850. Though for many decades his contributions were under - recognized internationally, modern historians and mechanicians consider him a pioneer whose work laid critical foundations for continuum mechanics, influencing generations of engineers and physicists.

name: Hertha Marks Ayrton
whatOpened: Hertha Ayrton improved understanding of the electric arc, studied ripple formation, and invented practical devices like the Ayrton fan. She broke gender barriers as the first woman in the Institution of Electrical Engineers, the first to read a paper to the Royal Society, and the first female recipient of its Hughes Medal.
short: Hertha Ayrton was a British electrical engineer, physicist, mathematician, and inventor whose research clarified the physics of electric arcs, contributed to fluid and wave mechanics, and led to practical inventions. She was a pioneering female scientist in institutions that traditionally excluded women.
long: Hertha Marks Ayrton was born Phoebe Sarah Marks on 28 April 1854 in Portsea, England. She was the third of eight children of Levi Marks, a Polish Jewish watchmaker, and Alice Theresa Moss, a seamstress. Her father died in 1861, and the young Sarah helped support her family. Her academic promise was nurtured by relatives who introduced her to science and mathematics. In she entered Girton College, Cambridge, where she studied mathematics, helped lead the choral society, founded a fire brigade, and earned a mathematics certificate. Because Cambridge did not award degrees to women at that time, she took an external examination at the University of London and earned a Bachelor of Science in 1881. Around this time she adopted the name “Hertha,” inspired by literature, and became agnostic. In the early 1880s Ayrton supported herself by teaching and embroidery, and she published mathematical problems in Educational Times . In she patented a line divider, a precision drawing instrument useful to artists, architects, and engineers - her first major invention. Hertha attended evening classes in electricity at Finsbury Technical College, where she met William Edward Ayrton, a physicist and electrical engineer. They married in 1885, and she took his surname. Her scientific career took off with work on the electric arc - an important lighting technology of the day. She investigated the cause of hissing and instability in arc lamps, showing experimentally that oxidation at the positive electrode explained the effects, and suggested design improvements. In she became the first woman elected to the Institution of Electrical Engineers and read her own paper, The Hissing of the Electric Arc , before the institute. Following this, she studied ripple marks formed by sand and water dynamics. In she became the first woman to read a paper before the Royal Society, presenting The Origin and Growth of Ripple Marks . In 1906 she was awarded the Hughes Medal of the Royal Society for her contributions to electric arc and ripple research - a rare recognition for women at the time. During World War I, Ayrton applied her understanding of fluid motion to design the Ayrton fan, a hand-operated device intended to clear poisonous gases from trenches. Although initially dismissed by the War Office, many fans were ultimately used at the front. Ayrton was also active in the women’s suffrage movement and helped found the International Federation of University Women and the National Union of Scientific Workers. Her influence extended into advocacy and community support for women in science. Hertha Ayrton continued her research and engagement with science until her death from blood poisoning on August at North Lancing, Sussex. She remains celebrated as a pioneering woman in engineering and physics whose scientific insights and inventions had lasting impact.

name: Mary Golda Ross
whatOpened: Mary Golda Ross was the first known Native American female engineer and one of the founding engineers of Lockheed’s secretive Skunk Works program. Her work included early aerospace design, calculations for spaceflight concepts, contributions to rocket and satellite projects, and planning for interplanetary missions - laying groundwork that influenced U.S. space exploration during the mid - 20th century.
short: Mary Golda Ross was an American aerospace engineer of Cherokee descent. She worked at Lockheed Corporation from until her retirement in and was a founding member of the Skunk Works team. Ross also worked to support women and Indigenous students pursuing careers in science and engineering.
long: Mary Golda Ross was born on August 9, in Park Hill, Oklahoma, as the second of five children in a family of the Cherokee Nation. She was a descendant of Cherokee Chief John Ross. Ross showed academic promise early and studied mathematics at Northeastern State Teachers’ College (now Northeastern State University), graduating with a bachelor’s degree in 1928. She later earned a master’s degree in mathematics from Colorado State Teachers College (now the University of Northern Colorado) in 1938. After teaching mathematics and science in Oklahoma schools and working for the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Ross moved to California in and was hired by Lockheed Corporation during World War II. At Lockheed she began by supporting aircraft design, including work on the high - speed fighter P - Lightning. Following the war, Lockheed kept Ross on staff and supported her further training; she obtained a professional engineering certification from the University of California, Los Angeles in 1949, making her the first known Native American woman to hold such credentials. She became one of the original engineers in Lockheed’s Advanced Development Projects (Skunk Works), the highly confidential group that advanced aerospace and missile technologies. Much of the work Ross contributed to remains classified, but available records show she helped develop requirements and designs for early space vehicles and contributed to studies of orbital flight and interplanetary travel. She was one of the authors of the NASA Planetary Flight Handbook Vol. III, which included guidance on spaceflight concepts. Ross continued at Lockheed until her retirement in 1973. After retiring, she devoted decades to encouraging young people, especially women and Native Americans, to pursue math and engineering careers. She was involved with the Society of Women Engineers and the American Indian Science and Engineering Society. In she participated in the opening of the Smithsonian’s National Museum of the American Indian. Ross passed away on April 29, 2008, just months before her 100th birthday. In she was honored by her depiction on a Native American dollar coin issued by the U.S. Mint.

name: Caroline Augusta Kennard
location: Portsmouth, New Hampshire & Brookline/Boston, Massachusetts, USA
whatOpened: Caroline Kennard was an American naturalist and women's rights advocate whose scientific interests included botany (especially ferns and mosses) and contributions to natural history in her community. She challenged prevailing scientific views about women's intellectual capacities in correspondence with Charles Darwin and worked actively in organizations promoting women's education and reform, using science as part of her advocacy.
short: Caroline Augusta Kennard was an American amateur scientist and reformer known for her involvement in natural history, contributions to scientific societies, and her correspondence with Charles Darwin challenging ideas about women's intellectual inferiority. She was active in women's organizations and supported scientific and educational reform.
long: Caroline Augusta Smith was born on January 15, 1827, in Portsmouth, New Hampshire, to James Wiggin Smith and Eliza Folsom. She married Martin Parry Kennard, a Boston businessman and abolitionist, in 1846, and the couple settled in Brookline, Massachusetts. Kennard developed interests in both natural history and social issues. She collected and studied plants such as ferns and mosses and was listed in an 1885 scientific directory for her botanical interests. She donated specimens, including fruit from her own rubber plant, to the Boston Society of Natural History in 1892. Kennard was active in several women's organizations, including the New England Woman's Club, where she served on the board and was vice-president in 1893. She also participated in the Association for the Advancement of Women and the Women's Educational and Industrial Union, writing and speaking on topics such as educational reform, the economic value of housework, and the employment of police matrons. In 1882, Kennard corresponded with Charles Darwin to challenge his views on women's intellectual inferiority, arguing that differences in opportunity, not innate ability, explained observed disparities. Darwin replied, engaging with her arguments. Kennard wrote a biographical work on Dorothea Dix published in 1888, compiling letters from those who knew Dix, and contributed various articles on social reform and prominent women of her time. Her sister later established the Caroline A. Kennard Scholarship in Science at Radcliffe College. Caroline Kennard died on October 24, 1907, in Boston. She is remembered for her engagement in science, contributions to natural history societies, and her advocacy for women's roles in science and society.
sources: Caroline Kennard, Wikipedia. en.wikipedia.org

name: Ruby Sakae Hirose
location: Kent & Auburn, Washington; Cincinnati, Ohio; later West Reading, Pennsylvania, USA
whatOpened: Ruby Hirose was an American biochemist and bacteriologist whose research on serums, antitoxins, blood clotting, allergies, and infectious diseases contributed to the scientific foundation for vaccine development, including work relevant to vaccines against infantile paralysis (polio). In 1940 she was recognized by the American Chemical Society for her accomplishments in chemistry at a time when few women were honored.
short: Ruby Sakae Hirose was a Japanese-American biochemist and bacteriologist. She earned her doctorate in biochemistry and conducted research on the biochemical mechanisms of blood clotting, allergies, and antitoxins, contributing to scientific understanding that supported vaccine development and immunological treatments.
long: Ruby Sakae Hirose was born on August 30, 1904 in Kent, Washington, USA, the second of six surviving children of Shiusaka and Tome Hirose, Japanese immigrants to the United States. She grew up in the White River area near Seattle and graduated from Auburn High School as part of the Nisei (second-generation Japanese-American) community. Hirose attended the University of Washington, where she studied pharmacy and pharmacology. She later pursued graduate studies in biochemistry at the University of Cincinnati, earning her Ph.D. in 1932 with research focused on the nature and action of thrombin, an enzyme central to blood clotting. A resulting scientific paper from this work was published in the American Journal of Physiology. During her university years she was active in the honor society Sigma Xi and served as vice-president of Iota Sigma Pi, a national women's chemical fraternity. After completing her doctorate, Hirose worked on biochemical research, including studies of blood clotting, allergies, antitoxins, and infectious disease mechanisms. She became a research scientist at the William S. Merrell Laboratories in Cincinnati, where she examined serums and antitoxins and improved pollen extracts used in allergy treatments. Her scientific contributions and research achievements led to her being one of ten women honored by the American Chemical Society in 1940. Hirose's work also contributed to foundational research that supported vaccine development efforts against infantile paralysis (polio) and other diseases, and she published studies in biochemistry and bacteriology. During World War II, members of her family were interned under U.S. government policies affecting Japanese-Americans, but she herself remained in Ohio and was not incarcerated. Ruby Hirose died from acute myeloid leukemia on October 7, 1960, in West Reading, Pennsylvania, at the age of 56. She was buried at Auburn Pioneer Cemetery in Auburn, Washington. Her scientific career stands as an example of contributions by women and Japanese-American scientists to biochemistry and vaccine-related research in the early 20th century.

name: Ynés Enriquetta Julietta Mexía
location: Washington, D.C.; San Francisco & Berkeley, California, USA;
whatOpened: Ynés Mexía was an American botanist and plant collector whose extensive fieldwork between 1925 and 1938 yielded more than 145 000 botanical specimens, including over 500 species new to science and at least one new genus (Mexianthus), greatly expanding knowledge of plant biodiversity in the Americas. She became one of the most accomplished plant collectors of her era despite beginning her scientific career in her early 50s.
short: Ynés Mexía was a Mexican-American botanist and explorer. Beginning formal botanical study at age 51, she organized and conducted numerous expeditions across Mexico, Central and South America, and Alaska, collecting an extraordinary number of plant specimens. Her work enriched herbarium collections worldwide and left a lasting legacy in botany.
long: Ynés Enriquetta Julietta Mexía was born on May 24, 1870, in Georgetown, Washington, D.C., to a Mexican father and an American mother. She spent much of her early life in Texas, Philadelphia, and Mexico City before relocating to San Francisco in 1908 after personal upheavals. Her early career included social work, but she later found her passion in the natural world and conservation. In 1921, at the age of 51, Mexía began taking courses in botany at the University of California, Berkeley, and joined groups such as the Sierra Club and the Save the Redwoods League, which fostered her interest in plant collecting. Mexía's serious botanical career began in 1925 with fieldwork in Mexico. She quickly became a prolific collector and explorer, undertaking expeditions throughout the Americas, including Alaska, Mexico, Central America, and South America. Her travels sometimes took her into remote and challenging regions, where she collected thousands of specimens, often working independently and overcoming the era's gender norms in science. Over her approximately 13-year botanical career, Mexía amassed over 145 000 specimens, including more than 500 species new to science, and at least 50 species named in her honor. She also collected what would become the first general botanical collection in Denali National Park (Alaska) and contributed specimens housed in major herbaria across the United States and Europe. A new genus of plants, Mexianthus, was named after her by botanists recognizing her discoveries. Mexía documented her travels and occasionally lectured and published on her experiences. Her work expanded scientific understanding of plant diversity and provided critical material for research and taxonomy. She died of lung cancer on July 12, 1938, in Berkeley, California. In her will, she left part of her estate to conservation organizations, reflecting her lifelong appreciation of nature.

name: Esther Miriam Zimmer Lederberg
location: Bronx, New York & Stanford, California, USA
whatOpened: Esther Lederberg was an American microbiologist and a pioneer of bacterial genetics. She discovered the lambda phage - a virus that infects Escherichia coli and became a foundational tool for studying gene regulation and recombination - and was instrumental in developing replica plating, a technique that transformed how bacterial mutants are isolated and analysed. She also contributed to understanding gene transfer between bacteria, including early work on the fertility factor F, and later directed the Plasmid Reference Center at Stanford University.
short: Esther Lederberg was a pioneering American microbiologist whose discoveries - including lambda phage, fertility factor F, and replica plating - laid crucial foundations for molecular genetics and microbial genetics. She conducted much of her work in the mid-20th century and directed Stanford's Plasmid Reference Center.
long: Esther Miriam Zimmer was born on December 18, 1922, in the Bronx, New York, to David Zimmer and Pauline Geller Zimmer, in a working-class Jewish family. She graduated from Evander Childs High School at age 16 and earned a bachelor's degree in genetics from Hunter College (City University of New York) in 1942. Originally interested in literature, she switched to biochemistry against discouragement from teachers who thought science would be difficult for a woman, and she worked as a research assistant at the New York Botanical Garden early in her career. She won a fellowship to study at Stanford University, where she completed a master's degree in genetics in 1946, working with prominent geneticists George Beadle and Edward Tatum. That same year she married Joshua Lederberg. After Stanford, she pursued her doctorate at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, which she completed in 1950 with research that led to some of her landmark discoveries. While at the University of Wisconsin, Lederberg isolated the lambda phage, the first temperate bacteriophage - a virus that can integrate into the host E. coli genome and replicate with it - providing a powerful tool for studying gene regulation and recombination. She also contributed to understanding specialized transduction and, in collaboration with Luigi Luca Cavalli-Sforza and Joshua Lederberg, helped identify the fertility factor F, an element important for DNA transfer between bacteria. With her husband she co-developed the replica plating technique in 1952, which used a velveteen surface to transfer colonies between media and made it possible to efficiently screen for bacterial mutants. In 1956, Esther and Joshua Lederberg were jointly awarded the Pasteur Medal by the Society of Illinois Bacteriologists for their work in bacterial genetics. Although her pioneering contributions were sometimes overshadowed, she remained a respected figure in the field. In 1959 she returned to Stanford University, where she became professor of microbiology and immunology and later founded and directed the Plasmid Reference Center, maintaining and cataloguing plasmids and transposable elements - important genetic tools in molecular biology - until her retirement. Esther Lederberg continued her scientific work and mentorship throughout her career. She divorced Joshua Lederberg in 1966 and later married Matthew Simon. She also had interests outside the laboratory, including music. Esther Lederberg died on November 11, 2006, in Stanford, California, at age 83. Her scientific legacy continues to be recognized for its foundational impact on microbial genetics and molecular biology.

name: Charlotte Emma Moore Sitterly
whatOpened: Charlotte Moore Sitterly was an American astronomer whose precise spectroscopic measurements of the Sun and elements created standard atomic data tables, foundational for astrophysics and spectroscopy.
short: Charlotte Moore Sitterly was an American astronomer known for her extensive spectroscopic research of the Sun and chemical elements. Her authoritative tables of atomic spectra and energy levels remain important reference works. She received major honors including the Annie Jump Cannon Award in Astronomy and, posthumously, the Bruce Medal.
long: Charlotte Emma Moore was born on September 24, 1898 in Ercildoun, Pennsylvania into a Quaker family. She earned a Bachelor's degree in mathematics from Swarthmore College in 1920 and immediately went to work as a human computer at the Princeton University Observatory, assisting Henry Norris Russell with calculations and beginning her career in astronomical research. In the mid-1920s she moved to Mount Wilson Observatory, where she worked on solar spectroscopy, analyzing spectral lines to identify elements in the Sun and determining properties such as sunspot temperatures. This work laid the groundwork for her lifelong focus on spectroscopic data. Moore continued her studies at the University of California, Berkeley, where she completed a Ph.D. in astronomy in 1931 with a thesis on atomic lines in the sunspot spectrum. After earning her doctorate she returned to Princeton as a research assistant, collaborating with Russell and co-authoring influential publications, including The Masses of the Stars in 1940. In 1945 Sitterly joined the National Bureau of Standards (now NIST), where she led projects to compile and organize atomic spectra and energy levels. Her meticulously assembled tables, including Atomic Energy Levels and Multiplet Tables of Astrophysical Interest, became standard reference works for astronomers and spectroscopists worldwide and are valued for their accuracy and completeness. In later years she also expanded her work into the ultraviolet spectrum, incorporating data from rocket-borne instruments. Sitterly married physicist Bancroft W. Sitterly on May 30, 1937, but continued to publish under her maiden name because most of her scientific reputation was established prior to her marriage. She authored and co-authored more than 100 scientific papers over her career. Charlotte Moore Sitterly received the Annie Jump Cannon Award in Astronomy in 1937 and was elected the first woman associate of the Royal Astronomical Society of Great Britain in 1949. She also received other honors including the William F. Meggers Award from the Optical Society of America. In 1968 she retired from the National Bureau of Standards but continued research at the Naval Research Laboratory. The Journal of the Optical Society of America issued a commemorative edition in her honor in 1988. Posthumously she was awarded the Bruce Medal of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific in 1990, and the asteroid 2110 Moore-Sitterly is named after her. Charlotte Moore Sitterly died on March 3, 1990, in Washington, D.C. Her work remains an enduring part of astrophysical spectroscopy and atomic data standards.

name: Marietta Blau
location: Vienna, Austria-Hungary (now Austria) & Mexico City; United States; Vienna, Austria
whatOpened: Marietta Blau was an Austrian physicist who pioneered the photographic nuclear emulsion method for detecting subatomic particles, producing techniques that became foundational for experimental particle physics.
short: Marietta Blau was an Austrian experimental physicist known for developing nuclear emulsion techniques to record particle tracks and for discovering "disintegration stars" - patterns caused by cosmic-ray interactions - which helped lay the groundwork for particle physics. Despite being nominated for the Nobel Prize, she did not receive it in her lifetime.
long: Marietta Blau was born on April 29, 1894 in Vienna, Austria-Hungary, into a middle-class Jewish family. She showed an early interest in mathematics and physics and studied at the University of Vienna, where she earned her Ph.D. in 1919 with a thesis on the absorption of gamma rays. After graduation, Blau struggled to find paid research positions and worked in industry and university research settings before becoming an unpaid scientist at the Institute for Radium Research of the Austrian Academy of Sciences in Vienna in 1923. There she developed and refined photographic emulsion techniques - a method of using thick, specially treated photographic plates to detect charged particles and analyze their energy and identity. By measuring the paths (tracks) left by particles in the emulsion, she was able to distinguish between alpha particles, protons and background events, a major advance in experimental nuclear physics. In the 1930s, Blau and her collaborator Hertha Wambacher exposed nuclear emulsion plates at high altitude near Innsbruck, Austria, capturing tracks from cosmic rays. In 1937, they discovered distinctive "disintegration stars" - multiple particle tracks radiating from single points, resulting from cosmic rays breaking up heavy nuclei. These findings drew international attention and helped demonstrate the value of nuclear emulsions for particle physics research. Because of her Jewish heritage, Blau was forced to leave Austria after the Anschluss (annexation by Nazi Germany) in 1938. She first went to Oslo, Norway, then, with assistance from Albert Einstein, secured a position in Mexico City, where she worked in difficult conditions but survived the war years. In 1944 she moved to the United States, working for the U.S. Atomic Energy Commission and later at institutions including Brooklyn National Laboratory and the University of Miami, applying her emulsion techniques to particle research with accelerators. Blau's contributions to the photographic detection of particles were recognized by peers: she received the Ignaz Lieben Prize from the Austrian Academy of Sciences and was nominated twice for the Nobel Prize in Physics by Erwin Schrödinger, though she never won it. Advances in accelerator and detector technology eventually overshadowed emulsion methods, but her early work remained historically important. She returned to Vienna in 1960, where she continued to be involved in physics until her health declined. Marietta Blau died in Vienna on January 27, 1970 of cancer. Her pioneering role in experimental particle physics and the development of nuclear emulsion techniques has since been acknowledged as a key step in the evolution of the field.

name: Annie Jump Cannon
whatOpened: Annie Jump Cannon created a stellar spectral classification system (O, B, A, F, G, K, M) that became the universal standard for categorizing stars by temperature and remains foundational in astronomy.
short: Annie Jump Cannon was an American astronomer whose work classifying hundreds of thousands of stars and devising the Harvard spectral classification system significantly shaped modern stellar astronomy. She catalogued stars in the Henry Draper Catalogue and received many honors, including honorary degrees and medals.
long: Annie Jump Cannon was born on December 11, 1863 in Dover, Delaware, USA to Wilson Lee Cannon and Mary Elizabeth Jump Cannon. She was encouraged by her mother to study the stars from a young age. Cannon attended Wellesley College, where she studied physics and astronomy, and graduated in 1884. She undertook further studies in astronomy at Radcliffe College before beginning her professional career. In 1896 Cannon joined the Harvard College Observatory under director Edward C. Pickering as part of a group of women astronomers tasked with cataloguing stellar spectra. Early in her work she focused on variable stars, discovering more than 300. Cannon's major scientific contribution was the development of a simplified and systematic spectral classification scheme for stars. Building on earlier systems, she organized stellar spectra into the sequence O, B, A, F, G, K, M, arranged by decreasing temperature. This system replaced more complex earlier schemes and was later adopted by the International Astronomical Union in 1922 as the standard method for classifying stars. Cannon personally classified over 225,000 stars for the Henry Draper Catalogue published in multiple volumes, and continued extending the work through later catalogues. At Harvard, Cannon became Curator of Astronomical Photographs in 1911 and was made an honorary member of the Royal Astronomical Society in 1914. She received numerous honorary degrees during her career, including one from the University of Oxford, where she was the first woman to be so honored. Cannon also received awards such as the Henry Draper Medal from the National Academy of Sciences. She established the Annie Jump Cannon Award in Astronomy to support women astronomers. Cannon retired in 1940 but continued her research until shortly before her death. She died on April 13, 1941, in Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA. Her legacy endures through the classification system she devised, catalogues that remain reference works, and annual awards in her name.

name: Wang Zhenyi
whatOpened: Wang Zhenyi was a Chinese astronomer and mathematician who improved understanding of celestial motion, eclipses, and trigonometry and demonstrated scientific principles through experiments and writings despite restrictions on women's formal education in her time.
short: Wang Zhenyi was a Qing dynasty Chinese scientist, astronomer, mathematician, and poet. She wrote authoritative works explaining eclipses, the movement of stars and planets, and mathematical concepts in accessible language. Her legacy is commemorated by a crater on Venus named in her honour.
long: Wang Zhenyi was born in 1768 in Nanjing during the Qing dynasty into a learned family; her grandfather had an extensive library and encouraged her education. She studied astronomy, mathematics, and geography from family members and pursued self-directed learning in these subjects. Zhenyi became proficient in astronomy and mathematics at a time when formal scientific study by women was uncommon. She wrote several scientific texts: on the motion of stars and planets, the explanation of lunar eclipses, and mathematical topics such as trigonometry. She is noted for conducting an experiment to demonstrate how a lunar eclipse works using simple apparatus - a lamp, mirror, and table - to model the relative positions of the Sun, Earth, and Moon. Her writings helped clarify celestial mechanics for a broader audience. In mathematics, Wang rewrote difficult texts in simpler language, notably publishing The Simple Principles of Calculations, which made concepts like multiplication and division more accessible. She also analysed the shape of the Earth, arguing against flat-Earth notions, and advanced ideas on calendars and celestial motion. Beyond science, Wang Zhenyi was a poet, expressing progressive views on education and women's roles in her work. Her broad interests and intellectual achievements made her a remarkable figure in her era. Wang Zhenyi died in 1797 at about age 29. Her scientific contributions continued to be recognised posthumously; in 1994, the International Astronomical Union named a crater on Venus after her, commemorating her impact on astronomy.

name: Tapputi-Belatekallim
whatOpened: Tapputi-Belatekallim is the earliest recorded chemist known from written history, credited with developing ancient techniques of perfume extraction, distillation, and filtration.
short: Tapputi-Belatekallim was an ancient Mesopotamian perfume maker and palace overseer whose name appears on cuneiform tablets from around 1200 BC. She used solvents, repeated distillation, and filtration to create fragrances from flowers, oils, and resins - representing some of the earliest known chemical practices.
long: Tapputi-Belatekallim is known from cuneiform tablets dated to about 1200 BC in Babylonian Mesopotamia, where she is described as a perfumer and overseer of a palace - the meaning of the title Belatekallim. Her name and work are recorded in Akkadian, the language of administration in ancient Mesopotamia. The surviving inscriptions indicate that Tapputi used flowers, oil, calamus, cyperus, myrrh, balsam, and other aromatic materials in her preparations. She combined these with water or other solvents and employed multiple rounds of distillation and filtration to produce refined fragrances. The tablets that mention her also record her techniques, making her one of the first individuals in history whose chemical methods are documented in writing. Her work not only involved creating scented oils and balms but also demonstrates early use of still and solvent extraction methods - procedures that are recognized as precursors to later chemical practices. These techniques were innovative for her time and represent some of the earliest known examples of systematic material transformation and purification. Tapputi was not only a craftsman but also held responsibilities at the palace, implying she oversaw the production and possibly the training or management of others involved in scent preparation, including collaborators whose names partially appear on tablets. The archaeological record from Babylonian sites places her activity within elite contexts where perfumed oils had cultural, ritual, and possibly medicinal significance. Very little personal information survives about her life beyond what is preserved in these tablets. No firm details about her birth, family, or death are available in historical records. What remains most significant is that her name and techniques endure as the earliest known written reference to a practitioner we would today recognize as a chemist. Tapputi-Belatekallim's legacy is remembered as a pioneering figure in early chemistry and perfumery, illustrating how ancient scholars and artisans applied systematic methods to extract and refine substances long before formal scientific disciplines were defined.

name: Ellen Gleditsch
location: Mandal & Oslo, Norway; Paris & New Haven, USA
whatOpened: Ellen Gleditsch was a Norwegian radiochemist whose work on establishing the half-life of radium and her contributions to the experimental evidence for isotopes made key advances in early nuclear chemistry.
short: Ellen Gleditsch was a pioneering Norwegian radiochemist, one of the first women in her field and Norway's second female professor. She worked with Marie Curie in Paris and later founded radiochemistry research at the University of Oslo, and she was active in promoting women's scientific careers.
long: Ellen Gleditsch was born on 29 December 1879 in Mandal, Norway into a family that valued education and scientific curiosity. Although she excelled in school, women were not admitted to university entrance exams in Norway at the time, so she trained as a pharmacy assistant and completed a non-academic degree in chemistry and pharmacology in 1902. In 1905 she passed the university entrance exam with the support of her mentor and chose to study in Paris, where she joined the Curie Institute and worked with Marie Curie from 1907 to 1912. There Gleditsch carried out fractional crystallisations to purify radium, earning a Licenciée en sciences degree from the Sorbonne in 1911. After returning to Norway, she helped establish radiochemistry research at the University of Oslo and, in 1913-14, worked at Yale University with Bertram Boltwood, where she conducted measurements that helped establish the half-life of radium - a reference standard for many years. In 1929 she became Norway's second female full professor, leading radiochemistry and inorganic chemistry. Gleditsch was active in scientific and women's organizations. She co-founded the Norwegian Women Academics' Association, served as president of the International Federation of University Women, and worked to promote international scholarships for women scientists. Throughout her career she published research in multiple languages and maintained international collaborations, including trips to research centers in Europe and the United States. During World War II she supported colleagues under threat from Nazi persecution and continued to contribute to science and society after the war. Ellen Gleditsch retired in 1946 and later worked with UNESCO on educational initiatives. In 1962 she received an honorary doctorate from the Sorbonne, the first woman to be so honoured by that university. She died on 5 June 1968 in Oslo, Norway.
sources: "Ellen Gleditsch," Wikipedia. Wikipedia.

name: Fe Primitiva del Mundo
whatOpened: Fe del Mundo was a Filipino pediatrician whose lifelong work transformed child healthcare in the Philippines by founding its first pediatric hospital and advancing community-oriented pediatric care.
short: Fe del Mundo was a pioneering Filipina pediatrician and National Scientist of the Philippines. Her innovations in pediatric care, community health, and medical education elevated standards of child healthcare nationally and influenced practices in other countries.
long: Fe Primitiva del Mundo y Villanueva was born on 27 November 1911 in Intramuros, Manila, Philippine Islands. She was one of eight children of Bernardo del Mundo, a prominent lawyer, and Paz Villanueva. The early loss of siblings to childhood illnesses inspired her to pursue medicine, especially the health of children. Del Mundo enrolled at the University of the Philippines College of Medicine in 1926 and graduated as valedictorian with a medical degree in 1933. She passed the medical board exam the same year, ranking among the top examinees. She continued her postgraduate training in the United States, earning a Master of Arts in Bacteriology (1940) from Boston University and completing a research fellowship at Harvard Medical School's Children's Hospital from 1939 to 1941. Shortly before the start of World War II, del Mundo returned to the Philippines. During the Japanese occupation she volunteered with the International Red Cross, caring for children interned at the University of Santo Tomas camp, where she earned the nickname "The Angel of Santo Tomas" for her dedication. Later the city government appointed her head of a children's hospital that evolved into the North General Hospital (now Jose R. Reyes Memorial Medical Center). After the war, del Mundo joined academia, becoming head of the Department of Pediatrics at Far Eastern University for over two decades. In 1957 she founded the Children's Memorial Hospital in Quezon City, the first pediatric hospital in the Philippines; it later became the Fe del Mundo Medical Center, a major institution for child healthcare. Del Mundo's research spanned communicable diseases such as dengue, polio, and measles, and she authored more than 100 scientific articles as well as the Textbook of Pediatrics, which became a foundational medical reference in the Philippines. She also promoted community health programs including nutrition, immunization, family planning, and rural health education, and developed innovations such as a bamboo incubator for use in areas without electricity. Throughout her career she broke many barriers: she became the first Filipino woman to be admitted to Harvard Medical School's pediatric program, the first Filipina National Scientist (1980), and held leadership roles including president of the Philippine Pediatric Society and the Medical Women's International Association. Del Mundo continued her service to children and medicine into her late years, living at the hospital she founded and rounding on patients until near the end of her life. She died on 6 August 2011 in Quezon City, Philippines, at age 99 and was interred at the Libingan ng mga Bayani. Her legacy endures in institutions she created and the countless children and healthcare professionals she influenced.

name: Hazel Gladys Bishop
location: Hoboken, New Jersey & Rye, New York, USA
whatOpened: Hazel Bishop was an American chemist and entrepreneur who invented the first long-lasting, smudge-proof lipstick, transforming cosmetic chemistry and consumer beauty products.
short: Hazel Bishop was an American chemist, inventor, and businesswoman best known for creating "no-smear" or long-lasting lipstick and founding the cosmetics company Hazel Bishop, Inc. Her product quickly became a beauty industry staple and reshaped consumer expectations for lipstick performance.
long: Hazel Gladys Bishop was born on August 17, 1906, in Hoboken, New Jersey, USA, one of two children of Henry and Mabel Bishop. She showed an early interest in chemistry and went on to study at Barnard College, graduating with a B.A. in chemistry in 1929. After graduation she took graduate courses in chemistry at Columbia University while working as a research assistant in dermatological laboratories. From 1935 to 1942, Bishop worked as an assistant to a dermatologist in the Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons, gaining experience with cosmetics research. She then served as an organic chemist at the Standard Oil Development Company during World War II, where she worked on chemical formulations related to aircraft fuel, and later at Socony Vacuum Oil Company in a similar role through 1950. In the late 1940s, Bishop began conducting experiments to create a lipstick that would not smudge, dry, or smear, working with staining dyes, oils, and waxes in her own kitchen. After extensive experimentation she perfected a formula using bromo acid dyes that stained the lips rather than simply coating them. The result was marketed as "Lasting Lipstick". In 1948 she founded Hazel Bishop, Inc. to manufacture her lipstick, which debuted in stores in 1950, selling out immediately at major retailers such as Lord & Taylor. The product quickly captured a significant share of the U.S. lipstick market and prompted competing formulations from other cosmetic companies. Bishop served as president of her company until disputes with majority stockholders led to her departure in 1951-1954. After leaving her namesake company, Bishop continued developing consumer chemical products through Hazel Bishop Laboratories, including leather cleaners and solid perfumes. In the 1960s she transitioned into finance, becoming a registered broker and financial analyst specializing in cosmetics and related industries. Later, in 1980, she accepted a position at the Fashion Institute of Technology in New York as the Revlon Chair of Cosmetics Marketing, contributing to academic programs combining science, marketing, and beauty. Hazel Bishop died on December 5, 1998, in Rye, New York, at the age of 92. She is remembered for her pioneering contributions to cosmetic chemistry, her entrepreneurial success, and her influence on the beauty products industry.

name: Alice Catherine Evans
whatOpened: Alice Catherine Evans was an American microbiologist whose research demonstrated that Brucella abortus from unpasteurized milk caused brucellosis in humans, which was pivotal in promoting milk pasteurization and improving public health.
short: Alice Catherine Evans was a pioneering American microbiologist who established the link between a cattle bacterium and human brucellosis, leading to widespread adoption of milk pasteurization. She was the first woman elected president of the Society of American Bacteriologists (now American Society for Microbiology).
long: Alice Catherine Evans was born on January 29, 1881 in Neath, Bradford County, Pennsylvania, USA to William Howell Evans and Anne B. Evans. She grew up on a farm and was the younger of two children. After teaching school for several years, Evans pursued higher education. She attended the Susquehanna Collegiate Institute and later enrolled at Cornell University, where she earned a Bachelor of Science in bacteriology in 1909. She continued her studies at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, becoming the first woman to receive a graduate scholarship in bacteriology there, and earned her Master of Science in 1910. Evans began her scientific career in 1910 at the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Bureau of Animal Industry (USDA), where she was the first woman to hold a permanent research position. She investigated bacteriology in milk and dairy products, focusing on bacteria that caused disease in cattle. Through meticulous research, Evans showed that Brucella abortus - then known as Bacillus abortus - caused brucellosis (also called undulant fever or Malta fever) in both cattle and humans, transmitted through unpasteurized milk. Her findings, published in 1918, helped overturn earlier assumptions and ultimately influenced the widespread adoption of milk pasteurization in the United States by 1930, significantly reducing incidence of the disease. In 1928, Evans was elected as the first woman president of the Society of American Bacteriologists, a historic milestone for women in science. In recognition of her work and leadership, the American Society for Microbiology later established the Alice C. Evans Award for Advancement of Women in microbiology in her honor. Evans also received multiple honorary degrees during her career, including honorary doctorates from the Woman's Medical College of Pennsylvania, the University of Wisconsin-Madison, and Wilson College. She was a fellow of the American Association for the Advancement of Science and served as honorary president of the Inter-American Committee on Brucellosis. After retiring in 1945, Evans continued to be active in supporting science and women scientists. She donated her papers to the National Library of Medicine in 1969. Alice Catherine Evans died on September 5, 1975, in Alexandria, Virginia, USA. Her work remains foundational in microbiology and public health.

name: Ellen Ochoa
whatOpened: Ellen Ochoa became the first Hispanic woman in space, flying on four Space Shuttle missions and later leading NASA's Johnson Space Center, expanding representation and leadership in human spaceflight.
short: Ellen Ochoa is an American engineer, former astronaut, and space center director. She flew on four Space Shuttle missions, logged more than 40 days in orbit, earned patents for optical systems, and served as the first Hispanic director of NASA's Johnson Space Center.
long: Ellen Ochoa was born on May 10, 1958, in Los Angeles, California, USA. She grew up in La Mesa, California, and showed academic talent from an early age. Ochoa earned a Bachelor of Science in physics from San Diego State University in 1980, then went on to complete a Master of Science (1981) and Doctorate in electrical engineering (1985) at Stanford University, where she specialized in optical systems for information processing. She worked as a research engineer at Sandia National Laboratories and at NASA's Ames Research Center, where she investigated optical inspection and object-recognition systems and received three patents for her work. In 1990 she was selected by NASA as an astronaut candidate and completed training the following year. In April 1993, aboard Space Shuttle Discovery's STS-56 mission, Ochoa became the first Hispanic woman to travel to space, conducting experiments to study the Sun and Earth's atmosphere. She went on to fly on three additional missions - STS-66 (1994), STS-96 (1999), and STS-110 (2002) - contributing to atmospheric science and the assembly of the International Space Station and logging more than 40 days in orbit over the four flights. After her spaceflights, Ochoa served in NASA leadership roles. She became deputy director of Flight Crew Operations and later deputy center director at the Johnson Space Center. In 2013 she was appointed the 11th director of the Johnson Space Center, becoming its first Hispanic director and the second woman to lead the center, a position she held until her retirement from NASA in 2018. Throughout her career Ochoa has received numerous honors, including NASA's Distinguished Service Medal, and has served as a role model and advocate for STEM education, inspiring students - especially from Hispanic and underrepresented communities - to pursue science and engineering. Ellen Ochoa continues to engage in science and education initiatives, and her legacy includes patents, technical publications, and contributions to human space exploration.

name: Margarita Salas Falgueras
whatOpened: Margarita Salas developed methods using the φ29 DNA polymerase enzyme to replicate very small amounts of DNA quickly and reliably, a foundation for modern DNA amplification techniques used in genetics, forensic science, oncology, archaeology, and molecular biology.
short: Margarita Salas was a Spanish biochemist and molecular geneticist who pioneered DNA replication research in Spain, isolated and characterized the φ29 DNA polymerase enzyme, and became an internationally recognized leader in molecular biology. She received numerous national and international awards and was a role model for women in science.
long: Margarita Salas Falgueras was born on 30 November 1938 in Valdés (Asturias), Spain. She grew up in an educated family; her father was a psychiatrist and her mother a teacher. She studied chemistry at the Universidad Complutense de Madrid, earning her degree and later completing her Ph.D. in Sciences in 1963 under the supervision of Alberto Sols. Her doctoral work and growing interest in molecular biology led her to pursue postdoctoral training. After completing her doctorate, Salas worked for three years at New York University with Nobel laureate Severo Ochoa, where she gained important experience in molecular biology. She and her husband, fellow scientist Eladio Viñuela, returned to Spain in 1967 and established the country's first research group in molecular genetics at the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC) in Madrid. At CSIC, Salas studied the bacteriophage φ29, a virus that infects bacteria, and discovered that it produced an enzyme now known as φ29 DNA polymerase, which could replicate DNA with exceptional speed and accuracy. She isolated and characterized this enzyme and demonstrated its usefulness in amplifying tiny amounts of genetic material - a breakthrough that enabled reliable DNA analysis in many scientific fields. Salas and her team patented the use of φ29 DNA polymerase in the United States (1989) and Europe (1997). The patent became one of the most profitable in the history of the Spanish National Research Council, widely used in biotechnology and genetic testing. Her scientific contributions extended beyond φ29 DNA polymerase. Early in her career she also helped elucidate basic principles of DNA replication, including mechanisms of genetic information transfer and identifying features such as stop codons in transcription - foundational insights into molecular genetics. Throughout her career, Salas published hundreds of scientific articles, supervised doctoral students, and registered multiple patents. She held prominent positions at the Centro de Biología Molecular Severo Ochoa (CBM) of the CSIC, served as a professor at the Universidad Complutense de Madrid, and was elected to several academies including the Royal Spanish Academy and the American Academy of Arts and Sciences. Salas received many awards, including the European Inventor Award (2019) for lifetime achievement, the L'Oréal-UNESCO Award for Women in Science, the Mendel Medal, the Rey Jaime I Prize, the National Ramón y Cajal Research Award, and the Echegaray Medal, reflecting both her scientific impact and her pioneer status for women researchers. Margarita Salas continued working passionately in science throughout her life. She died on 7 November 2019 in Madrid, Spain, at age 80, leaving a lasting legacy in biochemistry and molecular genetics and inspiring generations of scientists worldwide.

name: Joan Feynman
location: Queens, New York & Pasadena, California, USA
whatOpened: Joan Feynman was an American astrophysicist whose work on the solar wind, auroras, and sun-Earth interactions clarified causes of geomagnetic phenomena and improved models for predicting space weather hazards.
short: Joan Feynman was a pioneering American space physicist known for her research on solar wind, the origin of auroras, and developing predictive models for solar activity's impact on spacecraft and Earth's magnetosphere. She was the first woman elected as an officer of the American Geophysical Union and received NASA's Exceptional Scientific Achievement Medal.
long: Joan Feynman was born on March 31, 1927 in Queens, New York, USA. She grew up fascinated by science and was encouraged in her interest by her older brother, physicist Richard Feynman. Feynman earned a Bachelor's degree from Oberlin College and went on to complete her Ph.D. in physics at Syracuse University in 1958, with a dissertation on the absorption of infrared radiation in crystal lattices. She also conducted postdoctoral work at Columbia University. Much of her career was devoted to studying how the solar wind interacts with Earth's magnetosphere, the region shaped by Earth's magnetic field. In 1971 at NASA's Ames Research Center, she discovered that the presence of helium in the solar wind could be used to identify coronal mass ejections (CMEs) - eruptions of solar material that affect space weather and can cause geomagnetic storms. Feynman also demonstrated that auroras arise from the interaction between the solar wind's magnetic field and Earth's magnetosphere, clarifying the physical cause of these light displays. She developed predictive models for high-energy particles to assess space radiation hazards to spacecraft. Throughout her career she held research positions at the High Altitude Observatory, the National Center for Atmospheric Research, the National Science Foundation, Boston College, and, from 1985 until her retirement, as a senior scientist at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Pasadena, California. In 1974, Feynman became the first woman elected as an officer of the American Geophysical Union (AGU) and organized committees to advance the fair treatment of women in geophysics. She was also a longstanding member of the International Astronomical Union. In 2002 she was awarded NASA's Exceptional Scientific Achievement Medal. Feynman authored or co-authored more than 100 scientific publications and edited several books. After retiring in 2003, she continued to publish research on solar variability's influence on climate. Joan Feynman died on July 21, 2020 in Ventura, California, USA at the age of 93. Her legacy endures in space physics and solar-terrestrial research.

name: Ruby Violet Payne-Scott
location: Grafton, New South Wales & Sydney, Australia
whatOpened: Ruby Payne-Scott was an Australian physicist and radio astronomer who helped establish radio astronomy as a scientific field, including the first radio interferometry observations of the Sun's radio emission.
short: Ruby Violet Payne-Scott was a pioneering Australian radiophysicist and one of the world's first female radio astronomers. Her work at CSIRO in the 1940s contributed to understanding solar radio emission and laid foundations for future radio astronomy techniques.
long: Ruby Violet Payne-Scott was born on 28 May 1912 in Grafton, New South Wales, Australia to Cyril Payne-Scott and Amy (née Neale). She later moved to Sydney and completed her schooling at Sydney Girls High School with honours in mathematics and botany. She won two scholarships to the University of Sydney, where she studied physics, chemistry, mathematics, and botany. Payne-Scott earned a Bachelor of Science in physics in 1933, followed by an MSc in physics in 1936 and a Diploma of Education in 1938. In the late 1930s she conducted research on the effects of magnetic fields on biological processes and briefly taught secondary school before joining AWA Ltd in 1939, where she worked as an engineer before moving on to scientific research. In August 1941 Payne-Scott joined the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) Division of Radiophysics (now CSIRO), working on top-secret radar research during World War II and becoming an expert in aircraft detection systems. After the war, as Radar systems were repurposed for science, she turned to radio astronomy. In early 1946, Payne-Scott, Joe Pawsey, and Lindsay McCready used radar antennas at Sydney headlands to perform the first radio interferometry observations in astronomy, confirming that solar radio bursts originated from sunspots. She helped discover and characterise types of solar radio bursts (Type I and III) and contributed to early concepts of aperture synthesis, a technique that would later become central in radio astronomy. Despite her scientific contributions, personal circumstances and workplace rules limited her career. She married in 1944 but kept it secret because CSIR rules forbade married women from holding permanent positions; when her marriage became known she was downgraded to temporary status and ultimately resigned in 1951 when she became pregnant. After leaving research, Payne-Scott taught mathematics and science at Danebank School in Sydney from 1963 to 1974. She died on 25 May 1981 in Mortdale, New South Wales, Australia. Ruby Payne-Scott's legacy is honoured through awards and honours in Australia, including a Ruby Payne-Scott Medal and Lecture to recognise outstanding women in science, and even a Sydney ferry named after her.

name: Mary Lucy Cartwright
whatOpened: Mary Cartwright was a British mathematician whose work on nonlinear differential equations helped lay foundations for what later became known as chaos theory.
short: Mary Lucy Cartwright was a pioneering British mathematician known for her contributions to analysis and differential equations and for her early work that anticipated chaos theory. She was the first female mathematician elected a Fellow of the Royal Society and held significant leadership roles in mathematical societies.
long: Mary Lucy Cartwright was born on December 17, 1900 in Aynho, Northamptonshire, England to William Digby Cartwright, the local rector, and Lucy Harriette Maud Cartwright. Her early education was conducted by governesses, and she later attended boarding schools where her interest in mathematics was nurtured. In 1919 she began studying mathematics at St. Hugh's College, Oxford, where she was among only a handful of women pursuing the subject. She graduated in 1923 with a first-class degree, a notable achievement at a time when women were still rare in advanced mathematics. Cartwright spent several years teaching before returning to Oxford for graduate study. She completed her D.Phil. in 1930 with a thesis on The Zeros of Integral Functions of Special Types, supervised by influential mathematicians including E.C. Titchmarsh and with support from G.H. Hardy. After her doctorate she moved to Girton College, Cambridge, where she began a long and distinguished career. She became a fellow and director of studies in mathematics and later served as Mistress of Girton College from 1949 to 1968, becoming the longest-serving head in the college's history. Cartwright's research spanned several areas of mathematical analysis, including complex functions, cluster set theory, and differential equations. In the late 1930s she began a collaboration with mathematician J.E. Littlewood on nonlinear differential equations arising in radio and radar theory. Their work revealed intricate solution behaviors that presaged later developments in chaos theory - decades before the term became standard in mathematics and science. Her contributions were widely recognized: in 1947 she became the first female mathematician elected a Fellow of the Royal Society. She was also the first woman to serve on the Society's Council and the first woman to receive its Sylvester Medal in 1964. Cartwright served as President of the Mathematical Association (1951-1952) and of the London Mathematical Society (1961-1963), in both cases breaking gender barriers. She was awarded the De Morgan Medal in 1968 and appointed Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire (DBE) in 1969. Throughout her career she published more than ninety research articles, including important results in function theory and nonlinear dynamics. Even after formal retirement, she continued to travel, lecture, and contribute to mathematics through editing the collected papers of G.H. Hardy and mentoring younger researchers. Mary Cartwright died on April 3, 1998 in Cambridge, England at the age of 97, leaving a legacy as one of the foremost women mathematicians of her era, whose early insights helped shape modern understanding of dynamic systems.
